Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917029,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzAyOQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy._core","name":"component: numpy._core","color":"009800","default":false,"description":""},{"id":5987216361,"node_id":"LA_kwDOAA3dP88AAAABZN2r6Q","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/sprintable%20-%20C","name":"sprintable - C","color":"5319E7","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-06-15T20:26:13Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T11:27:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When given a string, it looks like `np.clongdouble()` is parsing the numeric values as `float64` and losing precision in the conversion.  For example, in the following, I expect `np.longdouble(s)` and `np.clongdouble(s).real` to give the same result:

```
In [73]: s = "0.333333333333333333333333"

In [74]: np.longdouble(s)
Out[74]: np.longdouble('0.33333333333333333334')

In [75]: np.clongdouble(s).real
Out[75]: np.longdouble('0.33333333333333331483')

``` 
Instead, `clongdouble()` appears to be parsing to `float64`, c.f.

```
In [78]: np.longdouble(np.float64(s))
Out[78]: np.longdouble('0.33333333333333331483')
```

This is on a Linux machine where the `long double` type is 80 bit extended precision.


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.0.dev0+git20240613.787ff0f
3.12.3 (main, Apr 25 2024, 11:11:06) [GCC 11.4.0]


### Runtime Environment:

input:

```
import numpy as np

s = "0.333333333333333333333333"

a = np.longdouble(s)
b = np.clongdouble(s) #will outout complex form r+cj where r is the real part and c is the complex part
c = np.clongdouble(s).real #real part only

print(a)
print(a.dtype)
print(b)
print(b.dtype)
print(c)
print(c.dtype)
```


output:

```
0.33333333333333333334
float128
(0.33333333333333331483+0j)
complex256
0.33333333333333331483
float128
```

The types output by dtype match the documentation (https://numpy.org/doc/stable/user/basics.types.html)
but still output the wrong result.

I am interested in trying to investigate and fix this as a first contribution to numpy. Do I just go ahead and submit a PR or do I need to do anything else to indicate I have volunteered to work on it?

add to the above, I think the source of the issue may be because the number is converted to type Py_complex via the function PyComplex_AsCComplex in the Macro STRING_TO_CFLOAT_CAST, which I think is used to auto-generate the functions to convert a string to various complex float types, including clongdouble

(see https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/v2.1.0/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/stringdtype/casts.c    from line 1152 )

according to the documentation here:  https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/complex.html#c.Py_complex
the Py_complex type consists of 2 doubles (1 for the real part and one for the imaginary part)

The macro STRING_TO_CFLOAT_CAST then casts each part back to longdouble precision but it is too late as the precision has already been lost by converting to Py_complex.

This can be demonstrated by using cdouble to explicity convert to a complex nunber with double precisions, then convert that using longdouble. The result is identical to using clongdouble

e.g. 
input:
```

import numpy as np


numToTest = "0.333333333333333333333333"

a = np.longdouble(numToTest)
b = np.clongdouble(numToTest).real #real part only

#convet to cdouble then to longdouble
c = np.cdouble(numToTest)
c = np.longdouble(c)

print(a)
print(a.dtype)
print(b)
print(b.dtype)
print(c)
print(c.dtype)
```

result:
```

0.33333333333333333334
float128
0.33333333333333331483
float128
0.33333333333333331483
float128
```
I think a solution could be to add a condition to STRING_TO_CFLOAT_CAST to check for clongdouble cast type, and instead of the intermediate conversion to Py_Complex, use a different method for the conversion to the complex number in the case of clongdouble, that uses longdouble precision. 

Probably need to write a new subroutine to do the conversion -, can use string methods to split the string at the + and the j to get the numbers (still in string form), then parse each part to longdouble precisions using np.longdouble which keeps the precision
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2466145835/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
